IKoanRecipe + RecipeApplier (Koan.Recipe.Abstractions) — a 4th ordered-registration mechanism with ShouldApply gating; exactly 1 implementation (ObservabilityRecipe), zero external consumers

Consumers of manual path are test fixtures (Postgres/Json/SqlServer fixtures, DataCoreRuntimeFixture) + archived S2 sample; live S1.Web has it commented out

Jobs (IKoanJob<TSelf>) — durable at-least-once background work with its own triggers

Per-connector Infrastructure.Constants.Configuration.Keys constant trees with Alt* key aliases duplicating every key 2-4 ways

AI adapters: IReadOnlySet<string> Capabilities from AiCapability string constants + HasCapability(string) (IAiAdapter.cs:27)

AI config-level AiCapabilityConfig dictionaries on AiSourceDefinition/AiMemberDefinition — a third AI-side capability vocabulary

observations: Headline: 18 duplicate-concept clusters. 5 deliberate-layering, 7 drift, 6 unfinished-migration. Migration cost: 5 delete-only, 9 mechanical, 4 design-needed. The settled core (Data inner ring, Jobs, SEC-0002 auth) shows the collapse pattern WORKS — SEC-0002 §1 explicitly documents going 4-ways-to-1 and Data<T,K> documents 'the ONE execution path'. The drift lives almost entirely in pre-Facet-1 strata (AI capabilities, Messaging, Cqrs, Scheduling, Recipe, WebSockets).

Cheapest wins (pure deletion, zero consumers, verified by csproj/call-site grep): DataDirectAccess (0 call sites), Koan.WebSockets (0 src consumers), Koan.Data.Cqrs + its Mongo outbox connector (0 consumers), Koan.Rag (1 bootstrap-test consumer), Koan.Recipe.* (1 self-owned impl), TextPipeline/ImagePipeline (0 consumers). Removing these alone retires parts of 6 clusters.

The bootstrap authoring ratio is the single best longitudinal metric: 90 IKoanAutoRegistrar implementations vs 7 KoanModule subclasses (7.2% migrated). ARCH-0086 is additive-by-design so the interfaces stay, but the authoring-surface count should trend toward KoanModule; track 90:7 over time.

Self-admitted duplication exists in code comments: Postgres KoanAutoRegistrar.cs:51 ('The explicit AddPostgresAdapter() registration call already does this') — and the auto path already registers MORE than the manual path (discovery adapter, orchestration evaluator, Aspire), i.e. the two paths have already diverged, which is how dual registration becomes a correctness bug.

Framework dogfooding inconsistencies that will confuse consumers reading the source as documentation: EntityEndpointService injects IDataService AND calls Data<T,K> statics in the same class; Koan's own auth built-ins (RoleListFileContributor, AdminBootstrapContributor) still implement the legacy contributor interface their replacement deprecates; EntityVectorExtensions instance methods silently require the EnableWorkflows config flag while the static Vector<T> facade does not.

Verb synonyms are documented aliases, not accidents (Save 'alias for Upsert', Remove vs Delete), and partition-string overloads verifiably converge on EntityContext.Partition (Data.cs:406) — these are deliberate DX sugar over single paths, not parallel engines; the consumer-facing cost is API surface width (62 public statics on Entity<T>), not behavioral divergence.

Two pillar-level supersessions are complete in the new pillar but the old pillar still ships: Koan.Scheduling vs Jobs Schedule (JOBS-0005 has cron via Cronos; Scheduling's own README says cron is 'reserved for Phase 2'), and Cqrs outbox vs Jobs ledger transactional enlistment. Neither old pillar is marked obsolete anywhere; Koan.Web still hard-references Koan.Scheduling just to expose a /.well-known/scheduling endpoint.

The AI pillar is the largest un-migrated capability surface: one interface file (IAiAdapter.cs) simultaneously prescribes structural capability declaration AND string-set capability declaration, plus a third config-level AiCapabilityConfig vocabulary — three mechanisms in one subsystem while the rest of the framework standardized on ARCH-0084 CapabilitySet (26 implementors).

Three identically-named KoanServiceAttribute types in three namespaces (Orchestration / Web.Auth.Services / ServiceMesh) with incompatible constructors — currently harmless because their consumer sets don't intersect, but any app referencing two of the three pillars hits an ambiguous-type collision requiring alias gymnastics.
